ICU Team Update — July 2026
Midland Metropolitan University Hospital

ICU Team Update

July 2026 • Critical Care
🔬
Research Corner
Trials • Publications • Recruitment

A brilliant month across the board — every one of our four active trials is ahead of where it needs to be, and we have a major publication to share too. Thank you all for the part you've played in this.

Active Trials — July 2026
  • GenOMICC — Top 15 recruiting sites worldwide.
  • RELEASE — Top 8 in the UK for recruitment.
  • SHORTER — Recruiting over target every month.
  • ABBRUPT — Site recruitment target achieved this week. Great work all round.
May Recruitment Snapshot 8 patients recruited in May, from 27 screened. Of the remainder: 3 refused, 14 met exclusion criteria, 2 potential ABBRUPT patients were missed before treatment commenced, and 2 were ready for discharge before they could be approached for research.
🎯 Focus Areas for This Month Please complete and document P/F ratios for all ventilated patients, and highlight/escalate any P/F ratio below 20. Also remember to consider the ABBRUPT trial for patients with new-onset AF.
📋 Did You Know? — This Month's Clinical Pearl Stop using mucolytics (hypertonic saline and carbocisteine) in mechanically ventilated patients with acute respiratory failure — neither improves outcomes, and both cause harm (carbocisteine: roughly 6.5x the risk of significant upper GI bleeding; hypertonic saline: roughly 5x the risk of bronchoconstriction needing treatment, and roughly 13x the risk of hypoxaemia during nebulisation).

MARCH Trial Results — Published in NEJM

Hurray — the MARCH trial results are out, and huge congratulations to everyone in the SWB ICU and research team who made this happen. The trial tested whether the mucolytics carbocisteine and nebulised hypertonic saline help ventilated patients with difficult-to-clear secretions, recruiting 1,956 patients across 71 UK ICUs. Site PI: Dr Deepak Sharma.

MARCH Trial: Mucoactives in Acute Respiratory Failure — published in NEJM, 10 June 2026 (ISRCTN17683568).

The headline: neither drug shortened time on the ventilator, reduced ICU or hospital stay, or improved survival — and both carried harm. Carbocisteine was linked to a roughly 6.5x higher risk of clinically important upper GI bleeding (1.4% vs 0.2%). Hypertonic saline roughly quintupled bronchoconstriction needing a bronchodilator (2.4% vs 0.4%) and increased hypoxaemia during nebulisation around 13-fold (4.1% vs 0.3%).

✅ Bottom Line We should stop using mucolytics (hypertonic saline and carbocisteine) in mechanically ventilated patients with acute respiratory failure; they don't work, and they cause harm. Read the one-pager (Trust SharePoint) →

REMAP-CAP Update

REMAP-CAP, the platform trial comparing treatments for Community Acquired Pneumonia (PI: Prof Jonathan Hulme), has also reported recent results. Among critically ill patients (aged 12 or older) with confirmed influenza, treatment with oseltamivir was found to be ineffective in reducing 90-day mortality compared with no antiviral, and carried a high probability of harm.

Thank you to all those involved with recruitment and looking after participants across both these trials — our contribution would not have been possible without everyone's support.

Watch: About the ABBRUPT trial

Clinical Updates
Guidelines • Protocols • Education
🏃️ Altered Airway Unity Change Please see a summary of pertinent points for all regarding the Altered Airway Unity change, on the whiteboard outside the coffee room. Thank you to Carol for her amazing whiteboard skills!
The Altered Airway Unity change whiteboard, outside the coffee room — covering how to trigger tracheostomy/laryngectomy care plans, the MDT proforma, and tube change/decannulation steps. Whiteboard artwork by Carol.
🪨 DAS 2025 Difficult Airway Trolley — New Training Video As part of the recent 2025 update to the DAS (Difficult Airway Society) Guidelines, an educational video has been made outlining the Difficult Airway trolleys on Critical Care. It is essential for all staff to be familiar with both the equipment and the procedure to follow in an airway emergency. The trolleys now contain updated laminated sheets of the DAS 2025 algorithms for reference in each zone.

Access the video via SPARC › Critical Care › Respiratory System, or via Vimeo →. Link to the updated 2025 DAS Guidelines →. Questions to wasim.mir@nhs.net.

Albumin (HAS) Prescribing — Summary of Trust Recommendations

Guidance has been issued on the use of intravenous human albumin solution (HAS) in critically ill adult patients.

✅ When Albumin IS Recommended
  • Large-volume paracentesis (>5L) in cirrhosis — 20% HAS, 100ml per 2.5L drained.
  • Small-volume paracentesis (<5L) in cirrhosis — consider the same dosing, but discuss with a senior clinician and document clearly before prescribing.
  • SBP in cirrhosis — 20% HAS, 1.5g/kg within 6 hours of diagnosis, then 1g/kg on day 3.
  • Hepatorenal syndrome — contact an appropriate specialist.
❌ When Albumin Is NOT Recommended
  • Hypovolaemia or sepsis with hypoalbuminaemia — not first-line, not for post-resuscitation correction. Consultant authorisation required if used, with name and rationale documented.
  • Hypoalbuminaemia without ascites, SBP, or hepatorenal syndrome.
  • Alongside diuretics for extravascular fluid removal.
  • In patients on RRT for intradialytic hypotension or ultrafiltration.
Key message: albumin has a narrow evidence-based role in liver disease. Outside these indications, it should not be used routinely and requires explicit consultant sign-off.

📢 Newsletter Submission Reminder If you have updates worth sharing with the whole team, please add them using the submission form rather than via email or WhatsApp. It keeps everyone informed. Submit an update →
🤝 Supporting Patients & Families — ICUsteps ICUsteps (icusteps.org) is the intensive care patient support charity, providing guides covering the ICU stay, leaving hospital, returning home, delirium, tracheostomy, and more — available in 19 languages. QR codes are available at the bedside and are shared with patients and families on admission. icusteps.org →
Mandatory Training Please check that your mandatory training is current. If anything has lapsed, complete it promptly.

🛡️
Safety & Quality
Governance • Audit • Patient Safety
🧠 Liaison Psychiatry — Referral & Escalation Pathways As part of ongoing service improvement work between ITU and Liaison Psychiatry, the referral and escalation pathways for patients requiring urgent psychiatric input have been reviewed and clarified.
  • Standard referral response time is up to 24 hours. Where there is significant concern about patient safety, capacity, suicide risk, or safeguarding, consultant-to-consultant escalation is available in and out of hours.
  • Patients awaiting assessment who are considered at risk should have appropriate safeguarding measures in place, including enhanced observation where indicated.
  • In hours: the consultant covering Birmingham and Solihull Mental Health Trust is Dr Kozara Nader (0121 507 3416). If unavailable, the Liaison Psychiatry team or admin staff can provide an alternative contact.
  • Out of hours: contact the on-call Consultant Psychiatrist via BSMHT switchboard (0121 301 5500) — ask to be connected to the on-call consultant covering MMUH.
  • Consideration may be given to Section 5(2) where clinically appropriate and following senior medical review, to safeguard patients pending assessment.

Key message: clear escalation pathways exist in and out of hours — use them whenever there is significant concern about risk, capacity, patient safety, or the management approach.
💉 Peripheral IV Vasopressor Use — Updated Guideline An updated guideline on the use of vasopressor agents by peripheral intravenous infusion in adult critical care patients is now on MyDoc/Connect. This local guideline differs from national guidance, which is worth highlighting to rotating clinicians who may be familiar with alternative concentrations.

Within this Trust, and agreed by the ICU clinical governance group, a different concentration of noradrenaline and adrenaline (20 micrograms/ml) is used than recommended by the Intensive Care Society (10 mcg/ml) — although it sits within the range used in the evidence ICS cites. The higher concentration was chosen to limit infusion changes given the potential for patient instability, established local practice, and consistency with similar specialties across the UK.

CVC insertion should be the first option if safe peripheral administration is compromised, when rapidly escalating or higher doses are needed (>0.1 micrograms/kg/min), or when unable to wean down within 3 days. Read the full guideline →
🧪 PTH vs Procalcitonin — Check Your Request A recent increase in parathyroid hormone (PTH) tests being sent with routine morning bloods has been noticed. On review, many appear to have been requested in error, likely due to confusion with procalcitonin (PCT). Please ensure the correct test is selected — PTH and PCT serve very different clinical purposes, and inappropriate requests can lead to unnecessary cost and delays in care. If unsure, check with a senior member of the nursing or medical team before sending the sample.

ICU Stats Panel — Organ Donation

Organ and tissue donation and transplantation activity for 2025/26, reported on a rolling 12-month basis:

Full Breakdown — 2025/26
  • 5 consented donors → 4 actual solid organ donors → 7 patients received a transplant
  • 12 corneas donated to NHSBT Eye Banks
  • 115 patients referred to NHSBT Organ Donation Services — 100% referral rate, no missed referrals (exceptional compared with the national average)
  • 33 of those met UK Potential Donor Audit inclusion criteria
  • A Specialist Nurse was present at 11 of 12 donation discussions with families
  • Midlands ODR opt-in: 37% (vs 43% nationally)
  • Midlands-wide: 575 people received a transplant in 2025/26; sadly, 55 died on the waiting list
